author.name: The Value Trader
author.description: Full Time Technical Analyst & Investor | This is not financial advice

kept.text:
GoTo earnings mentioned $SE's ShopeeFood. They believe they have a stronger value proposition but not really convinced by the response 😂 Basically admitted they got boxed into a premium niche.

“Now moving on from there, right? If I talk about how do we think of competition and how have we been competing. So I think 2 things come to mind. First,  on food delivery, we constantly get user feedback, where we hear that in the affluent market, we are significantly preferred more, which is more and more visible based on the significant growth that we've seen on the 30-minute delivery that we order on food. And for the affluent base, we are much more stronger than Shopee or the other competitors. Mass market, we are trying to build up a user base and figure out how do we grow there sustainably and we're building out a lot of new capabilities, which help us compete better. So where we are today is a tale of 2 cities. We have 1 affluent where we're doing really well. And I think we are very strong versus the competition. We have the other segment of users where we are growing, not fully there, but we really expect to get there in some time. I hope that helps answer your question.”

author.name: TacticzHazel
author.description: Long-Term Investor | Focus on Quality Fundamental Analyses and Insights | Join 3000+ readers: https://t.co/XZyrRNC7WI |  https://t.co/hbCC9qnb42

tweet.text:
$MELI market share in LATAM: 37%
$SE market share in SEA: 53%

This is surprising to see, considering $MELI had a 15-20 year head start on $SE. Yet, I believe this is not representative of the ability of each company's management team and pace of innovation.

$SE certainly accelerated its growth through learning from incumbents like $BABA, $AMZN, $MELI but I think it is much more nuanced than that.

These are the reasons I believe $SE has larger market share than $MELI in its respective regions:

1. $MELI was a first mover, $SE was a second mover.

When $MELI started in 1999, everything had to be built from scratch, payments, logistics, and most importantly the trust layer in low-trust economies.

Meanwhile, Shopee launched in 2016 when smartphones were already widespread, consumers were comfortable with using apps, and logistics/payments infra already existed (partially).

2. $SE Shopee was far more aggressive with subsidies and gamification.

Shopee flooded the market with incentives such as free shipping, huge vouchers/cashback and a gamified UX, built through a mobile-first design from day one. This created a powerful demand flywheel where users came for discounts, sellers followed the traffic, and selection improved, reinforcing user growth.

$MELI in contrast was more infrastructure-first and less aggressive in burning capital early. Shopee's strategy enabled it to scale much faster in users and GMV especially in price-sensitive SEA markets.

3. China supply chain tailwind

This was and remains a massive advantage for Shopee. China's e-commerce market has been several years ahead of other regions, with a huge seller market. This, combined with existing cross-border supply chains and low-cost manufacturing, enabled Shopee to source a wide range of products at much lower prices.

$MELI historically had more localised supply and had to solve domestic fulfilment market by market. Interestingly, MELI is now trying to close this gap, opening 16 new fulfilment centres in 2025, including one in China.

4. Mobile-first v Desktop-first

This is perhaps the largest differentiator. $MELI started in a desktop era, where only the affluent had access. This creates structural inertia, where product design, user flows and even organisational thinking are influenced by legacy assumptions.

$SE was designed for mobile-native users from day one. Its interface, gamification features, and engagement loops are optimised for short attention spans and high-frequency app usage. This aligns closely with consumer behaviour in Southeast Asia, where mobile is the primary internet interface.

TLDR; Shopee captured Southeast Asia faster because it entered later, with better timing, more capital aggression, mobile-first design and had access to China's supply chain.

author.name: Gab
author.description: I write about asymmetric investments that go unnoticed, with a deep focus on emerging markets.

quoted_tweet.text:
eMarketer modeling Brazil ecom market share by GMV for 2025 at ~37% $MELI, 15% $SE and ~11% $AMZN.

Looks like Amazon and Shopee swapped places this year. I expect in 2026 MELI to be closer to 40%, Shopee to be closer to 20%, Amazon to be stagnant and legacy retailers to lose more share.

author.name: CapexAndChill
author.description: Long-term value in high conviction holdings. All thoughts are my own. NFA

tweet.text:
Just incase you think we're in a bubble, go look back at some of the valuations in 2021, they'll make some of the names today look like legacy companies.

$LMND hit a peak of $160.7
$SE hit a peak of $357 
$SNOW hit a peak of $392 
$VUZI hit a peak of $25+ 

Just to name a few. Yes they may have been growing fast (not sure about VUZI) but look at how reality caught up to each of these companies. 

A great thought exercise based on relatively recent price action that shows us valuations can get extremely ahead of themselves and take years to recover. 

$LMND is a far better company now but only in the mid $60's for example. Extremely important to understsnd what you own and really breakdown valuations.
